title: The End of the Meat Computer: How Karpathy’s Autoresearch is Automating the Future of AI
text:
Transformer-Architecture-Infra


By March 2026, the history of artificial intelligence will likely be divided into two eras: the era of the “meat computer” and the era of the autonomous swarm.
For decades, frontier research was gated by the biological constraints of human engineers—individuals who required sleep, sustenance, and the cumbersome ritual of “group meetings” to synchronize data via low-bandwidth sound wave interconnects (speech). This manual, high-latency approach is rapidly becoming a relic. In the envisioned future, research is the sovereign domain of AI agents operating across compute cluster megastructures, iterating at a velocity that renders human intervention not only unnecessary but impossible.
Andrej Karpathy’s autoresearch repository is the “Patient Zero” of this transition. It is a lightweight, provocative demonstration of a world where the human role shifts from writer to architect, and the code itself becomes a living, self-optimizing organism.

Takeaway 1: Research as a Self-Modifying Loop
The core of autoresearch is a continuous, closed-loop evolutionary process. While the human sleeps, an AI agent—acting as a autonomous researcher—applies relentless evolutionary pressure to a single file: train.py.
This is not a simple hyperparameter sweep. In this framework, everything is fair game. The agent can modify the neural architecture, swap out the training loop, or rewrite the optimizer logic (which defaults to a sophisticated mix of Muon and AdamW). After each modification, the agent triggers a training run and measures its success against val_bpb (validation bits per byte).
Because val_bpb is vocab-size-independent, it acts as a universal fitness function, allowing the agent to objectively compare wildly different architectural experiments. If a change improves the metric, it is assimilated; if not, it is discarded. This represents a fundamental climb up the abstraction ladder: we are no longer optimizing the model; we are optimizing the process that optimizes the model.


Takeaway 2: The Radical Philosophy of the 5-Minute Constraint
The most profound architectural decision in the project is the “Fixed Time Budget.” Every training run is hard-capped at exactly five minutes of wall-clock time.
By holding time constant rather than total training steps, Karpathy forces the AI to solve for efficiency on actual hardware. This constraint yields approximately 12 experiments per hour, or 100 per night, providing two major advantages:

Direct Architectural Comparability: Whether the agent increases the model’s depth or alters the batch size, the question remains the same: What is the best possible loss achievable in 300 seconds? This makes disparate experiments instantly comparable.
Hardware-Specific Evolution: The agent is forced to discover the most efficient utilization of the specific silicon it inhabits (such as an NVIDIA H100). The resulting code isn’t just “good”; it is an emergent property of the available compute.



Takeaway 3: The Human as the “Research Org” Architect
In this new paradigm, the human engineer stops writing Python and starts “programming the program.” The repository logic is partitioned into three distinct layers:

prepare.py: The bedrock. It handles data downloading, BPE tokenization, and utility functions. It is static and serves as the environment in which the research occurs.
train.py: The agent’s sandbox. Karpathy chose to keep the model, optimizer, and training loop in a single file to keep the scope manageable and make the agent’s “diffs” easily reviewable by the human orchestrator.
program.md: The human’s lever. This is the only file the human modifies. It contains the high-level instructions, “skills,” and meta-strategies for the agent.

The human’s job is now to iterate on the program.md instructions to find the “research org code” that produces the fastest progress. You aren’t coding a model; you are coding a researcher.


Takeaway 4: Emergence Within the Megastructure
One surprising consequence of hardware-specific evolution is that the “best” model becomes uniquely tailored to its environment. A model optimized on a single H100 may differ significantly from one evolved on a consumer-grade 4090. While this makes results less comparable across different users, it ensures that each model is perfectly tuned for its specific “compute cluster megastructure.”
This is the beginning of software that is no longer portable in the traditional sense, but rather “native” to the hardware that birthed it. By focusing on a “single GPU, single file, one metric” approach, the project strips away the bloat of distributed training to focus on the pure velocity of autonomous discovery.


The Forward-Looking Summary
The autoresearch repository is a window into a future where software is a “story of how it all began.” We are moving toward a reality where we might look at the “10,205th generation of a codebase” and find something unrecognizable.
As the agent-led iterations accumulate, the code will inevitably transform into a self-modifying binary that has grown beyond human comprehension—a black box of peak efficiency that no “meat computer” could have designed from first principles. We are transitioning from an era of manual instruction to an era of objective-driven evolution.
The future of AI is no longer being written; it is being evolved.




 Back to top

Run quarto render

Fix

title: The Hidden Physics of the Transformer
text:
The most revealing problems in computer science are often not computational problems at all. They are physical ones.
This observation has appeared repeatedly throughout the history of computing, although it is easy to overlook because software has a peculiar habit of concealing the machinery beneath it. An algorithm is written as though memory were infinite, processors instantaneous, and communication free. Reality, of course, is less accommodating. Every instruction ultimately becomes the movement of electrical signals through silicon, every data structure occupies a finite location in memory, and every computation competes with countless others for access to buses, caches and interconnects. The elegance of an algorithm on paper is therefore only one half of the story. The other half is whether the underlying machine can execute it efficiently.
Large language models have reached precisely this point in their evolution.
For much of the past decade, discussion surrounding transformer architectures has been dominated by mathematics. Attention mechanisms, positional encodings, normalization strategies, activation functions and scaling laws have understandably occupied the spotlight. They are intellectually satisfying subjects because they describe how a model learns. Yet there is another, quieter discipline that determines whether those ideas can be deployed at scale: computer architecture.
It is difficult to appreciate this distinction until one considers a simple question.
What, exactly, is a GPU doing while a language model generates the next token?
The intuitive answer is that it is performing matrix multiplications. This is certainly true, but it is incomplete in much the same way that describing a modern city as “a place where people work” is true without explaining traffic, electricity, water distribution or public transport. Computation is only one activity occurring within the machine. An equally significant amount of time is spent retrieving operands from memory, transferring intermediate results between processing units, synchronizing execution across thousands of concurrent threads and waiting for data that has not yet arrived.
The larger a model becomes, the more these invisible activities begin to dominate.
This phenomenon is neither unique to artificial intelligence nor particularly new. Computer architects have spent decades confronting what has become known as the memory wall . Processor performance has historically improved at a substantially faster rate than memory bandwidth and latency. Arithmetic units capable of performing trillions of floating-point operations per second are of limited value if they must repeatedly pause while waiting for the data upon which those operations depend. A processor deprived of information resembles an assembly line deprived of components: its theoretical productivity becomes largely irrelevant.
The implications for transformer models are profound.
Consider inference rather than training. Every generated token requires the model to revisit information accumulated throughout the conversation. Hidden states from previous tokens are transformed into keys and values and stored within the key-value cache, allowing subsequent attention operations to reuse earlier computations rather than recompute them from scratch. Conceptually, this is an elegant optimization. Computationally, it introduces an entirely different problem.
The cache grows monotonically with context length.
A conversation containing a few hundred tokens presents little difficulty. A conversation spanning hundreds of thousands of tokens does. Every newly generated token now requires reading an increasingly large collection of keys and values distributed across high-bandwidth memory. The arithmetic required to compute attention may remain manageable, particularly following innovations such as FlashAttention, which dramatically improved memory access patterns during the attention computation itself. Yet before the GPU can perform those optimized computations, it must first retrieve the relevant information from memory. The cost of transportation begins to rival, and eventually exceed, the cost of calculation.
The distinction is subtle but fundamental.
Most discussions of transformer efficiency remain preoccupied with reducing the number of floating- point operations. Increasingly, however, FLOPs are not the scarce resource. Modern tensor cores are astonishingly efficient at multiplying matrices. What they cannot do is eliminate the physical distance separating computation from data. Information must still traverse the memory hierarchy, moving from high-bandwidth memory into on-chip SRAM, through shared memory and registers, before finally reaching the execution units responsible for computation. Every journey consumes time. Every journey consumes energy. Every unnecessary journey compounds across billions of tokens.
One might reasonably ask why these issues appear so suddenly. After all, transformer architectures have existed since 2017. Why did memory bandwidth emerge as the defining bottleneck only recently?
The answer lies in an irony familiar throughout the history of engineering. Success often creates the conditions under which entirely new limitations become visible.
Early transformer models were sufficiently small that arithmetic dominated execution time. There were simply too few parameters and too little context for memory movement to become overwhelming. Simultaneously, GPU vendors were delivering extraordinary improvements in computational throughput. Tensor cores, mixed-precision arithmetic and increasingly sophisticated compiler optimizations caused the cost of matrix multiplication to fall far more rapidly than the cost of moving data. The consequence was predictable, though not immediately obvious. Computation became progressively cheaper while communication remained stubbornly constrained by the physical realities of semiconductor manufacturing.
The bottleneck migrated.
A useful analogy can be found in transportation engineering. Imagine a city that responds to growing traffic congestion by continuously building faster automobiles while leaving its roads unchanged. Initially, travel times improve. Eventually, however, the limiting factor ceases to be the speed of individual vehicles and becomes the capacity of the road network itself. At that point, constructing an even faster engine contributes almost nothing to the average journey. Progress instead requires redesigning intersections, optimizing traffic flow and reducing unnecessary travel altogether.
The transformer finds itself in a remarkably similar situation.
For years, the community devoted extraordinary effort to building increasingly powerful computational engines. Larger models, faster tensor cores and more capable accelerators delivered spectacular gains. Yet these improvements gradually exposed another reality. The dominant cost was no longer performing computation but supplying computation with the information it required. Intelligence, one might say, had become inexpensive. Transporting intelligence had not.
This observation represents the first genuine departure from the scaling paradigm described in the previous chapter. If memory movement rather than arithmetic has become the principal constraint, then entirely different optimization strategies become attractive. Compressing representations becomes more valuable than accelerating computation. Locality becomes more valuable than raw throughput. Numerical precision becomes an architectural decision rather than merely a statistical one. Entire classes of innovations—many of which initially appear modest when viewed in isolation— suddenly reveal themselves as responses to a common underlying problem.
This is precisely the context in which several seemingly unrelated developments begin to converge. FlashAttention reorganizes the order in which attention is computed to minimize unnecessary transfers between high-bandwidth memory and on-chip memory. Grouped Query Attention reduces the amount of key-value state that must be stored and retrieved during inference. FP8 arithmetic reduces the volume of information flowing through the memory hierarchy. Compiler technologies fuse kernels to eliminate intermediate writes back to memory. Even distributed training strategies increasingly prioritize overlapping communication with computation, recognizing that idle hardware often reflects unavailable data rather than insufficient arithmetic capability.
None of these techniques attempts to make transformers more intelligent.
They attempt to make transformers less wasteful.
Viewed independently, each innovation appears incremental. Viewed collectively, they reveal something more significant. The center of innovation had shifted away from inventing fundamentally new learning algorithms toward understanding where the machine itself was leaking efficiency. Modern AI research was beginning to resemble high-performance computing, a discipline in which success is rarely achieved through a single dramatic breakthrough and far more often through relentless elimination of hidden overhead.
The consequences of this change extend beyond hardware utilization. Once engineers begin viewing computation as a scarce economic resource rather than an abundant one, every layer of the transformer becomes open to reconsideration. Why should every parameter participate in generating every token? Why should every representation remain stored at full precision? Why should every communication operation block useful work elsewhere in the system? Questions that once seemed peripheral suddenly become central.
It is here that one encounters perhaps the most consequential architectural idea of the modern transformer era. Surprisingly, it is also one of the oldest.
Long before anyone imagined trillion-parameter language models, researchers had proposed a deceptively simple idea: perhaps intelligence does not require every part of a network to think all of the time.
The idea was called Mixture of Experts .
For nearly three decades it remained an elegant concept waiting for the surrounding ecosystem to catch up.



 Back to top

title: The Cost of Learning
text:
In the closing years of the nineteenth century, the American psychologist  entity  [“people”,“Hermann Ebbinghaus”,“psychologist”]  undertook an unusual experiment. Frustrated by the absence of rigorous methods for studying memory, he decided to make himself both the researcher and the subject. Over several years, Ebbinghaus memorized thousands of meaningless syllables—carefully constructed sequences such as WID , ZOF and BOK —only to measure how quickly he forgot them. His work produced what is now known as the forgetting curve, one of the earliest quantitative descriptions of human learning.
Hidden within those experiments was an observation that extended well beyond psychology.
Learning is expensive.
More precisely, relearning is expensive.
A student who repeatedly studies material they have already mastered wastes time that could have been spent acquiring genuinely new knowledge. Effective education therefore depends not merely on presenting information, but on identifying what no longer needs to be taught.
For more than a decade, large language models have behaved like remarkably diligent but strangely inefficient students.
Every token in a training corpus receives essentially the same treatment. Every example contributes another gradient update, whether the model already understands the underlying concept or not. A paragraph explaining Newton’s laws may reinforce knowledge the network acquired millions of examples earlier, yet the optimization process has little choice but to process it again. Stochastic gradient descent is wonderfully general, but it possesses no explicit notion of curriculum, mastery or educational efficiency. It simply continues learning until the loss declines.
When models contained hundreds of millions of parameters, this inefficiency was acceptable.
When training corpora expanded into tens of trillions of tokens, it became impossible to ignore.
The economics are sobering. Every additional training token consumes storage bandwidth, networking bandwidth, accelerator time and electrical power. The cost is incurred whether the example teaches the model something genuinely new or merely repeats a lesson it has already internalized. From the perspective of optimization theory this redundancy is unsurprising. From the perspective of engineering economics, it increasingly resembles waste.
This realization marks another quiet shift in the history of frontier AI.
Earlier chapters argued that researchers gradually discovered waste in computation, memory movement, communication and numerical precision. The next frontier proved more subtle. The waste was no longer hidden within the hardware.
It was hidden within learning itself.
The first signs appeared in an unexpected place.
Traditional autoregressive language models are trained to predict precisely one token at a time. Given a sequence of text, the model estimates the probability distribution of the next token, computes the loss, updates its parameters, and then repeats the process billions of times. The objective is mathematically elegant and has produced extraordinary results, yet it also discards a surprising amount of information.
Imagine asking a student to complete a paragraph while grading only the very next word they write.
The student still learns.
But much of the information contained within the remainder of the sentence is simply ignored.
This observation motivated one of the more interesting ideas to emerge from recent frontier research: Multi-Token Prediction .
Rather than supervising only the immediate next token, the model is encouraged to predict several future tokens simultaneously. The architecture extracts substantially richer learning signals from exactly the same training example. No additional documents are collected. No additional GPU clusters are constructed. The model simply learns more from every token it already processes.
The improvement appears modest when described algorithmically.
Economically, it is profound.
Once again, capability increases not because additional computation has been purchased, but because existing computation has become more informative.
This philosophy reappears throughout modern reasoning models.
For many years, supervised fine-tuning occupied a privileged position within the language-model pipeline. Researchers collected carefully curated demonstrations, trained models to imitate those demonstrations, and only then applied reinforcement learning as a final stage of refinement. The procedure was effective, but expensive. High-quality supervised datasets require enormous human effort to construct, continually age as new domains emerge, and often teach the model exactly one preferred solution to a problem.
Recent systems have begun questioning this assumption.
DeepSeek’s R1 demonstrated that remarkably capable reasoning behaviour could emerge through reinforcement learning with comparatively little reliance on large supervised reasoning corpora. Rather than memorizing vast collections of human-produced chains of thought, the model learns by interacting with reward signals that favour successful reasoning trajectories. The distinction is subtle but important. Supervised learning tells the model how humans solved the problem . Reinforcement learning increasingly asks the model to discover its own effective strategies within a constrained objective.
This should not be misunderstood as the abandonment of supervised learning.
Rather, it represents another example of engineering maturity.
The question has shifted from Which learning paradigm is superior? to Which learning paradigm extracts the greatest amount of useful capability from every unit of computation and every unit of human effort?
The same logic underlies the growing importance of distillation.
Training frontier models from first principles has become an undertaking measured in millions of GPU- hours and enormous financial investment. Yet once such a model exists, its knowledge need not remain confined to its original architecture. Smaller models can learn by imitating the behavioural distributions of larger ones, acquiring capabilities that would otherwise require vastly greater computational expenditure. In educational terms, the frontier model becomes a teacher.
This changes the economics of intelligence in a fundamental way.
Knowledge itself becomes reusable.
The first model pays the full cost of discovery.
Subsequent generations increasingly pay only the cost of transmission.
Computer science has encountered this pattern before. Compilers encode decades of optimization research so that every programmer need not rediscover instruction scheduling. Numerical libraries encapsulate sophisticated algorithms behind simple interfaces. Databases preserve hard-won engineering knowledge accumulated over generations. Distillation performs a similar function for machine learning. Intelligence becomes something that can be transferred rather than repeatedly rediscovered.
One begins to notice a recurring pattern.
Sparse architectures ask whether every parameter must participate.
MLA asks whether every representation must remain fully materialized.
FP8 asks whether every bit must be preserved.
Multi-token prediction asks whether every training example can teach more.
Distillation asks whether every model must learn independently.
Different techniques.
The same question.
Where is the waste hiding?
This, perhaps more than any individual algorithm, characterizes the current phase of frontier AI research. Mature engineering disciplines eventually stop asking how to build larger systems and begin asking how to build systems that learn, remember, communicate and adapt more economically.
The language model is no longer merely an object being optimized.
The process by which it acquires intelligence has itself become an optimization problem.
Yet training, for all its expense, occurs only occasionally.
A frontier model may be trained once, distilled a handful of times and fine-tuned periodically throughout its lifetime.
Inference is different.
Inference happens every second.
Millions of users generate billions of requests, each demanding an immediate response. Every inefficiency that survives training is paid repeatedly for years afterwards. If training determines the cost of creating intelligence, inference determines the cost of living with it. And those two costs are rarely the same.



 Back to top

title: The Memory Wall
text:
There is an old observation in computer architecture that has survived nearly every technological revolution of the past half century.
Computers rarely become slower because they forget how to compute.
They become slower because they forget where they left their data.
The distinction appears almost trivial until one realizes how profoundly it has shaped modern computing. Every major leap in processor performance has eventually encountered the same invisible adversary. Arithmetic units become faster. Pipelines become deeper. Parallelism increases. Yet memory, governed by entirely different physical constraints, improves far more slowly. The processor spends an increasing fraction of its existence doing something profoundly undignified: waiting.
Computer architects gave this phenomenon a name decades ago.
The Memory Wall .
The phrase refers to the widening disparity between computational throughput and memory performance. As processors accelerated, the time required to retrieve data failed to keep pace. Eventually, computation itself ceased to be the dominant expense. The cost lay in moving information to the place where computation could occur.
History has repeated this lesson with remarkable consistency.
Supercomputers discovered it.
Database systems discovered it.
Scientific simulations discovered it.
And, somewhat belatedly, large language models discovered it as well.
At first glance, the transformer appears almost immune to such concerns. Its computational profile is dominated by dense matrix multiplications, precisely the sort of workload modern GPUs were designed to accelerate. NVIDIA’s Tensor Cores execute these operations at extraordinary throughput, often performing hundreds of trillions of floating-point operations each second. Looking only at theoretical compute capability, one might conclude that modern accelerators possess computational headroom to spare.
Yet GPUs, like all processors, cannot multiply numbers that have not yet arrived.
To appreciate the significance of this observation, it is worth considering what occurs during autoregressive inference. A language model does not generate an entire response simultaneously. It produces one token, then another, then another, each prediction conditioned upon everything that preceded it. Recomputing the entire attention mechanism for every previously generated token would be computationally absurd. Instead, transformers retain intermediate representations—keys and values —from earlier tokens within what has become known as the KV cache . Future attention operations simply reuse these stored representations.
Conceptually, the optimization is elegant.
Physically, it is expensive.
Every additional token extends the cache. Every subsequent prediction must retrieve progressively larger collections of keys and values before attention can be computed. Context windows that once contained a few thousand tokens have expanded to hundreds of thousands and, increasingly, beyond one million. The arithmetic required for attention has certainly grown, but another cost has expanded far more rapidly: memory traffic.
The crucial point is that the KV cache is not merely large.
It is repeatedly accessed.
Unlike model parameters, which remain comparatively static during inference, the KV cache grows continuously and must be revisited for every generated token. Each attention operation therefore initiates an intricate choreography through the GPU memory hierarchy. Data residing within High Bandwidth Memory must be transported across memory controllers into on-chip SRAM, distributed among streaming multiprocessors, loaded into registers, and only then can tensor cores begin performing the computations they were built to execute.
The computation itself may require only microseconds.
The journey often requires considerably longer.
This inversion surprises many engineers because discussions surrounding transformer efficiency frequently emphasize computational complexity while neglecting arithmetic intensity. Complexity describes how many operations an algorithm performs. Arithmetic intensity asks a different question: how much useful computation is obtained for every byte moved through memory? High-performance computing has long recognized that algorithms with poor arithmetic intensity frequently become bandwidth-bound regardless of their theoretical computational complexity.
Modern transformers increasingly belong to this category.
The remarkable success of FlashAttention illustrates this point beautifully.
FlashAttention did not fundamentally alter the mathematics of self-attention. Instead, it reorganized the computation so that intermediate results remained within fast on-chip memory whenever possible, dramatically reducing expensive transfers to and from HBM. The achievement was architectural rather than statistical. It acknowledged a reality that had become increasingly difficult to ignore: reducing memory movement frequently produces larger practical gains than reducing arithmetic.
Yet FlashAttention solved only part of the problem.
It optimized how attention was computed.
It did not substantially reduce what needed to be stored.
As context windows continued their relentless expansion, another question gradually became unavoidable.
Must every key and every value exist in its original dimensionality?
The question appears deceptively innocent.
Its implications are profound.
Machine learning has repeatedly demonstrated that high-dimensional representations frequently possess surprisingly low intrinsic dimensionality. Principal Component Analysis, autoencoders, latent diffusion models and countless other techniques exploit the observation that complex information often resides upon comparatively compact manifolds. The explicit representation appears enormous. The underlying information content frequently is not.
DeepSeek’s Multi-head Latent Attention extends precisely this intuition into the transformer itself.
Rather than storing full key and value tensors for every token, MLA first projects these representations into a substantially lower-dimensional latent space. During inference, the latent representation is retained instead of the original tensors. When attention is later required, keys and values are reconstructed from the compressed latent representation before computation proceeds.
At first encounter, the idea almost appears counterintuitive.
Compression is usually associated with loss.
Inference demands precision.
How can both simultaneously be true?
The answer lies in recognizing that the purpose of the KV cache is not faithful reconstruction in the traditional signal-processing sense. The cache exists only to preserve the information required for future attention computations. If the latent representation retains precisely those features that attention ultimately depends upon, reconstructing the original tensors exactly becomes unnecessary. The objective is functional equivalence rather than numerical identity.
This distinction transforms the economics of inference.
A smaller latent representation occupies less memory.
Less memory requires less bandwidth.
Reduced bandwidth allows higher throughput.
Higher throughput reduces serving cost.
Longer contexts become economically practical without requiring proportionally larger memory systems.
One architectural decision propagates throughout the entire inference stack.
What makes MLA particularly elegant is that it attacks the problem at its true point of origin. Previous optimizations largely accepted the existence of enormous KV caches and attempted to access them more intelligently. MLA questions whether those caches should have existed in their original form at all.
This is a recurring pattern throughout the evolution of computing.
Engineers initially optimize an expensive operation.
Eventually someone asks whether the operation itself is necessary.
The latter question almost always proves more consequential.
Importantly, MLA should not be understood as an isolated breakthrough emerging in intellectual isolation. It belongs to a broader movement within modern AI engineering toward representation efficiency. Grouped Query Attention reduced redundancy among attention heads. Quantization reduced numerical precision without significantly sacrificing model quality. FlashAttention minimized unnecessary memory transfers. Compiler technologies increasingly fused kernels to eliminate intermediate writes. Collectively, these developments reveal an industry slowly abandoning the assumption that abundant computation justifies abundant movement.
The shift is philosophical as much as technical.
For years, language model research implicitly treated memory as passive storage—a warehouse through which information simply passed on its way toward computation. Increasingly, memory has become an active participant in architectural design. Representations are compressed, reorganized, partitioned and scheduled with the same care previously reserved for neural network topology itself.
In hindsight, this evolution appears almost inevitable.
Once computation became inexpensive relative to communication, the next generation of innovation was always going to occur within the pathways connecting them.
Yet memory represents only one form of communication.
The moment a language model expands beyond a single accelerator, another, even more formidable challenge emerges. Information must now travel not merely across memory buses within one GPU but across thousands of GPUs connected by networks that, despite extraordinary engineering, remain painfully slow compared with the speed of computation occurring inside each device.
The Memory Wall, formidable though it is, turns out to be only the first wall.
Beyond it lies the network.



 Back to top

title: Author’s Note
text:
This is not an article about Chinese artificial intelligence.
Nor is it an argument about geopolitics, export controls, benchmark leadership, or the relative strengths of one research laboratory over another. Those discussions are important in their own right, but they are not the subject of these pages.
This is an essay about a recurring phenomenon in the history of computing.
Every technological era begins by believing it has discovered the fundamental currency of progress. In one generation it is transistor density. In another, processor frequency. Later it becomes the number of cores, the size of distributed clusters, or the amount of available memory. For a time, these relationships appear almost immutable. They are observed, measured, formalized and eventually accepted as natural laws.
Then something curious happens.
A new generation of engineers arrives, not because they possess superior mathematics or larger budgets, but because they ask questions that the previous generation gradually stopped asking. They inherit systems whose assumptions have become invisible through familiarity. Their advantage is not that they know more. It is that they no longer mistake engineering choices for the laws of physics.
The history of computing is filled with such moments.
The transition from vacuum tubes to transistors was not simply a change in materials; it was a change in what engineers believed was possible. The decline of single-core processors did not occur because clock speeds ceased to matter, but because another constraint—power density—quietly became more important. The rise of cloud computing was not merely the consequence of larger datacenters, but of recognizing that computation itself could be treated as a dynamically allocated resource rather than a fixed asset. Again and again, the field has advanced not because yesterday’s ideas were wrong, but because yesterday’s bottlenecks ceased to be tomorrow’s.
Artificial intelligence now appears to be passing through a similar transition.
For much of the past decade, the dominant narrative surrounding large language models has been one of scale. Larger datasets, larger clusters, larger models and larger computational budgets produced undeniably remarkable progress. This observation was both empirically correct and historically significant. Yet somewhere along the way, a useful description of how progress had occurred gradually hardened into an assumption about how progress must continue.
That assumption is now being questioned.
The laboratories discussed throughout this essay—DeepSeek, Qwen, Moonshot AI, MiniMax and others —did not simply build capable language models. More interestingly, they exposed a different way of thinking about the problem itself. Rather than treating the transformer as a finished invention whose capabilities depended primarily upon additional computation, they approached it as a complex computational system still rich with hidden inefficiencies. Memory movement, numerical precision, distributed communication, routing algorithms, compiler optimizations and hardware utilization became first-class research problems rather than implementation details.
Whether these particular models remain state of the art five years from now is almost beside the point.
The engineering ideas they represent almost certainly will not disappear.
If the argument presented here is correct, future historians of artificial intelligence may look back on this period not as the moment one laboratory overtook another, nor even as the beginning of reasoning models, but as the point at which frontier AI ceased to be viewed primarily as a machine learning problem and began to resemble something much older: a problem in computer architecture, distributed systems, numerical computing and the economics of computation itself.
That, ultimately, is the story this essay attempts to tell.
